mnemonics.title: BFS vs DFS Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: BFS = Breadth = Queue = Layers | DFS = Depth = Stack = Go Deep

mnemonics.title: Topological Sort with DFS
mnemonics.content: Remember: To sort topologically with DFS, think 'Push to Stack when DONE' - vertices are added to result after all their neighbors are explored.

mnemonics.title: Cycle Detection
mnemonics.content: In DFS, if you see an already-visited vertex that is NOT your parent, there's a cycle (undirected). In directed, a back-edge means cycle.

acronyms.usage: Explores level by level, uses Queue data structure, finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
Explores depth-first, uses Stack/recursion, used for cycle detection, topological sort, connectivity
A directed graph without cycles; only DAGs can have topological sorting
Number of nodes in a graph, used in time/space complexity analysis (O(V+E))
Number of connections between vertices in a graph

visualTips.visualization: Imagine ripples in a pond - BFS expands outward in concentric circles from the source vertex, visiting all nodes at distance d before distance d+1
Imagine exploring a maze - DFS goes as deep as possible along one path before backtracking to try other paths
Think of islands connected by bridges - each connected component is an island, bridges connect vertices within an island
Think of prerequisite courses - if course A must be taken before B, then A comes before B in the ordering

analogies.analogy: Like searching for someone in a building by checking floor by floor - you check all rooms on floor 1, then floor 2, etc.
Like exploring a cave system - you follow one path as far as it goes, mark it, then backtrack to find new passages
Like a line at a ticket counter - first person in line gets served first, new people join at the end of the line
Like a stack of plates - you can only add or remove plates from the top, last plate added is the first one removed
